 be the tell. The most watched senator in this category is, of course, Mitc=
h McConnell, who hasn=E2=80=99t actually said whether he=E2=80=99s retiring=
=2E I=E2=80=99m not hearing any specific n=
ames, but there is a sense that a s=
enator or two might quickly get their fill of the latest round of Trump dra=
ma and decide to hang
it up.

But in reality, Trump will probably mostly get his way in staffing the exec=
utive branch. Even with these possible waverers, it=E2=80=99s hard to see t=
he Senate issuing many rejections at the cabinet level, let alone for the l=
ower-tier nominees who will most likely also be like-minded Trump loyalists=
=2E

Musical Chairmanships

In the wake of November=E2=80=99s presidential loss, House Democrats have e=
ntered a period of generational upheaval. On Monday, 76-year-old Ra=C3=BAl =
Grijalva announced he would step down as the party=E2=80=99s ranking member=
 on the Natural Resources Committee; 77-year-old Jerry Nadler faces a serio=
us challenge from the charismatic Jamie Raskin to lead Dems on Judiciary; a=
nd 79-year-old David Scott=E2=80=99s party leadership on the Agriculture Co=
mmittee may also be in jeopardy=E2=80=94even as Farm Bill deliberations gro=
w more urgent by the day.

As I wrote ( https://puck.news/newsletter_content/kash-management-a-dem-lea=
dership-shake-up-jamie-raskin-fantasies/?utm_campaign=3DThe+Best+%26+The+Br=

htest+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2812%2F3%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=
=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) on Sunday, this all came on =
very suddenly. Democrats=E2=80=99 primary concern is the stewardship of the=
 committees. But a secondary frustration is political: Some Democratic comm=
ittee leaders did very little to help the party try to reclaim the House ma=
jority. Both parties put expectations on their chairs and ranking members t=
o bring in money to their campaign committees, with the implicit message be=
ing that the only way to get a chairman=E2=80=99s gavel is for the party to=
 win control of the
chamber. (To be clear: Nadler, for his part, did bring in money, and per pa=
rty documents, campaigned hard for colleagues as well.) But according to th=
e monthly D.C.C.C. dues sheet, many more-junior members have transferred mu=
ltiple millions of dollars to the committee=E2=80=94including Jasmine Crock=
ett, Robin Kelly, Raja Krishnamoorthi, Deborah Ross, and Haley Stevens. Muc=
h of this fundraising prowess and largesse toward the D-Trip comes from mem=
bers who were elected in the post-2016 world of the Trumpified G.O.P. As th=
ese members move up in the party, we might see them using their increasingl=
y powerful perches to further boost the committee=E2=80=99s receipts, and i=
t seems reasonable to anticipate they may grow frustrated with the bottlene=
cking for top committee assignments.

Finally, there=E2=80=99s an important changing-of-the-guard coming on the R=
epublican side of the House: Politico reported ( https://www.politico.com/n=
ews/2024/12/02/dan-conston-to-leave-chief-house-gop-super-pac-00192245?utm_=
campaign=3DThe+Best+%26+The+Brightest+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2812%2F3%2F24%29&utm_c=
ontent=3DThe+Best+%26+The+Brightest+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2812%2F3%2F24%29&utm_med=
ium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a )=
 yesterday that Dan Conston will leave the Congressional Leadership Fund=E2=
=80=94the powerful super PAC aligned with House Republican leadership. Cons=
ton is a key House Republican operative who=E2=80=99s spent his career spec=
ializing in House races, and he has few peers when it comes to institutiona=
l memory. He was also a major cog in Kevin McCarthy=E2=80=99s political mac=
hine. Because of the G.O.P.=E2=80=99s hard-money fundraising deficiencies i=
n 2024 (given the results this cycle, it=E2=80=99s gone underappreciated th=
at House Republican candidates shat the bed in fundraising
vs. their Democratic counterparts), Conston=E2=80=99s super PAC carried a m=
uch
heavier load in buying TV time than had previous iterations of the C.L.F.

Yes, the G.O.P. is on track to lose, in aggregate, a seat or two this cycle=
=2E But Conston and the C.L.F. were a m=
ajor reason Republicans narrowly held=20=
on to the House majority. Replacing Conston will be one of the most consequ=
ential political decisions facing Mike Johnson and House leadership in the =
new term.
